text: Religious factor in Indonesian foreign policy is frequently stated to have played only marginal roles in Indonesia's foreign policy by recent scholars. The period of the Indonesian Revolution, however, that is 1945-1949 testifies to the opposite. During a period of relative instability, that is weakness, of the Indonesian state, various non-state actors, that is nationalist student movements as well as overseas Indonesian communities particularly in the Middle East played a more important part in relations with the world outside. Making full use of the Islamist discourse of 'Islamic Brotherhood' the activists could secure recognition to Indonesian republic for 'Islamic' and consequently for the Arab League and also in Egypt de jure on 13 January 1949. Later, however, as the state acquired greater bureaucratic and physical power in the diplomatic area and at the same time was to concentrate on United Nations and Western powers also taking into account that there was in the meantime the 1948 war in Palestine, which distracted the people's attention towards more pressing problems of Islamic nature, the role of Islam had to decline to the margin in the official foreign policy-making process of Indonesia. This article is however no more than a short historical phase and is proved by showing the underlying structural relationship between religion and foreign affairs of a state which at any given time differs in structure of state-society relations. This is, that in a phase in which the society, in relation to a state, has the power to promote religious principles to various stages in the making and implementation of foreign policy, then the religion, here Islamist ideology of the period concerned, is considered an important and often key factor in the promotion of national interest, as it did for Indonesia during its Revolution period, which we may call the period of 'Sovereign Survival by Means of Islamic Solidarity'.
